title: Quiz: Quantum Singular Value Decomposition
description: Test your understanding of quantum SVD, low-rank approximation, and applications to PCA and recommendation systems

questions.question: How does quantum SVD extract singular values of matrix A?

options.text: Phase estimation on the Hermitian extension H_A = [[0,A],[A^dag,0]]

questions.explanation: Quantum SVD constructs the Hermitian extension H_A whose eigenvalues are plus/minus the singular values of A. QPE on H_A then extracts these as phases, revealing the singular values.

questions.question: What are the eigenvalues of H_A = [[0,A],[A^dag,0]] in terms of A's singular values?
questions.explanation: The Hermitian extension H_A has eigenvalues that come in pairs +/-sigma_j for each singular value sigma_j of A. This is because H_A^2 = diag(AA^dag, A^dag A) with eigenvalues sigma_j^2.

questions.question: What is the Eckart-Young theorem about low-rank approximation?

options.text: Any matrix can be decomposed into low-rank factors
The best rank-k approximation is the truncated SVD A_k = sum of top k singular components
Quantum computers can find better approximations than classical

questions.explanation: The Eckart-Young theorem states that the truncated SVD A_k (keeping only the k largest singular values and corresponding vectors) is the optimal rank-k approximation minimizing ||A - A_k|| in both Frobenius and spectral norms.

questions.question: What additional resource is typically required for quantum speedup in SVD applications?
questions.explanation: Many quantum linear algebra speedups, including quantum SVD, require QRAM for efficient state preparation from classical data in O(polylog(n)) time. Without QRAM, state preparation may take O(n) time, negating the quantum advantage.

questions.question: Quantum PCA can provide exponential speedup over classical PCA in all cases.
questions.explanation: False! Quantum PCA speedup depends on specific conditions: efficient state preparation (QRAM), low effective rank, good conditioning, and when sampling from the principal component distribution suffices (rather than extracting full components). Classical algorithms can match quantum in many practical cases.

questions.question: In quantum recommendation systems using SVD, what is the output?

options.text: A quantum state encoding the recommendation distribution
The full SVD factorization U, S, V

questions.explanation: Quantum recommendation algorithms output a quantum state from which recommended items can be sampled. This sampling-based output is key to the speedup - extracting all matrix entries would require O(n) measurements, negating the advantage.

questions.question: How does the condition number kappa affect quantum SVD complexity?

options.text: No effect - quantum SVD is independent of condition number

questions.explanation: Quantum SVD and related algorithms typically have O(kappa^2) dependence on condition number, similar to HHL. This is because success probability and precision requirements both scale with kappa. Ill-conditioned matrices significantly impact quantum algorithm performance.

questions.question: Which applications can benefit from quantum SVD? (Select all that apply)

options.text: Principal Component Analysis for dimensionality reduction

questions.explanation: Quantum SVD provides speedups for: (a) PCA via eigenvalue extraction from covariance, (b) recommendation systems via low-rank matrix approximation, (c) pseudoinverse computation using singular value inversion. Sorting (d) is unrelated to SVD and has different quantum algorithms.
